topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every carefully chosen anecdote, every carefully framed achievement, was a deliberate lie. My breath hitched, a sharp, painful intake of air that burned in my chest. I read the dates, the locations, the emotional arcs—all of it belonged to someone else, meticulously sculpted into the life I thought I possessed. A tremor ran through my hands, and I slammed the papers onto the mahogany desk, the sound echoing sharply in the silent office. "This is impossible," I managed, my voice tight, strained to a pitch it barely recognized. I stood up, pacing the length of the room, my jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscles protesting. I needed answers, and the silence of the room was deafening, amplifying the sheer audacity of the deception.

[story 2]

I stared at the glossy biography, the ink mocking me from the page. It detailed my triumphs, my supposed struggles, all spun into a narrative that was utterly foreign. A deep, cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward until my entire frame felt rigid. I didn't move for a long moment, just letting the weight of the revelation settle. When I finally spoke, the words emerged low and dangerously steady. "Who authorized this?" The tone was flat, devoid of warmth, but the intensity behind the question made the air crackle. I walked to the window, gripping the frame until my knuckles bleached white. Outside, the city lights blurred, mirroring the chaotic churning inside me. I felt a desperate need to tear the paper in half, to rip the falsehoods out of existence.

[story 3]

The sheer betrayal hit me like a physical blow. I sat back in my chair, my spine ramrod straight, refusing to let myself slump. My eyes scanned the text again, searching for the microscopic flaw, the tell-tale sign of forgery. Finding none, the realization washed over me—a profound sense of being utterly undermined. A slow, controlled tremor started in my hands, escalating into a restless tapping against the armrest. I pushed the chair back, the scrape of metal on tile grating against the quiet. "They fabricated this," I stated, the words clipped, each syllable weighted with suppressed energy. I started walking toward the door, my gait purposeful, every step radiating a coiled tension. I didn't care where I went; I only cared about exposing the architects of this elaborate deceit.
